title: Workbook — The Liturgy
html:
Student Workbook

For use after class or in a group. Print this page or work in your notebook.

1. Remembering the Essentials

  1. What does the word “liturgy” mean in its Greek origin, and how does the Catechism define it (CCC 1066-1070)?
  2. What are the three Persons of the Trinity and what does each one do in the liturgy? Give at least one specific action for each Person.
  3. What is the “total Christ” (“Christus totus”) and why is he the subject who celebrates the liturgy (CCC 1136)?
  4. What is the difference between the common priesthood of the faithful and the ministerial priesthood (CCC 1141-1143, 1546-1547)? How do they relate to one another?
  5. Name at least seven liturgical signs and symbols that the Catechism highlights and explain what each one signifies (CCC 1145-1162).
  6. What does it mean that the earthly liturgy is a participation in the heavenly liturgy (CCC 1090, 1137-1139)?
  7. What are the principal liturgical seasons and what does each one celebrate (CCC 1163-1171)?
  8. Explain the relationship between the Liturgy of the Word and the Liturgy of the Eucharist. Why are both essential and inseparable?

2. For Reflection and Writing

Answer each question honestly. There are no “right answers” to these personal questions.

1. Describe the last Mass you attended. What did you feel? What did you like and what was hard for you?

Use three adjectives to describe your experience:

___________ ___________ ___________

2. If you thought—truly believed—that at every Mass heaven opens and the angels and saints are present, would anything change about how you participate?

What concrete thing would change? (arriving earlier, dressing with more reverence, participating more attentively, preparing beforehand, receiving Communion more consciously...)

3. What is your favorite liturgical sign or gesture? (holy water, sign of the cross, kneeling, incense, Paschal candle, altar bells, silence, music, the elevation of the Host...)

Explain why that sign helps you connect with God. What does it communicate to you that words cannot?

4. What liturgical season are we in right now? How could you live it in a special way this week?

Look up the liturgical calendar if you don't know. Propose a small concrete action that fits this season.

5. The priest acts in persona Christi (in the person of Christ). Does this help you when you don't like the priest or the homily seems boring?

Why doesn't the liturgy's efficacy depend on how likable or holy the priest is? What does this tell you about the nature of the sacraments?

6. The Catechism says the liturgy is “the summit toward which the activity of the Church is directed” and “the font from which all her power flows” (CCC 1074). What does this mean in everyday terms?

If this is true, why do we often treat Sunday Mass as just one more thing on our to-do list?

7. When you receive Communion, do you think about what (who) you are actually receiving? Does the idea that you are receiving the Body, Blood, Soul, and Divinity of Jesus Christ change anything about how you approach that moment?

What would help you receive Communion more fruitfully?

3. Practical Activities

A. Draw Your Church… with Heaven Open

Take a blank sheet of paper. At the bottom, draw your parish as it is: the altar, the pews, the people, the priest. At the top, draw what Revelation describes: the Lamb on the throne, the angels, the saints, the light, the never-ending song of praise. In the middle, draw a staircase or a beam of light that unites the two halves. That is the liturgy: the bridge between heaven and earth. Write underneath: “In the earthly liturgy we share in the heavenly liturgy” (CCC 1090).

B. The Colors of the Year

Draw a circle divided into the seasons of the liturgical year. Color each section with its corresponding liturgical color. Next to each season, write one word that captures how you feel during that time, and one goal for living it better:

  • Advent (violet): feeling _________ | goal: _________
  • Christmastide (white): feeling _________ | goal: _________
  • Ordinary Time I (green): feeling _________ | goal: _________
  • Lent (violet): feeling _________ | goal: _________
  • Eastertide (white): feeling _________ | goal: _________
  • Ordinary Time II (green): feeling _________ | goal: _________

C. Your Ideal Mass (with Eyes Wide Open)

In 10 lines, describe what your ideal Mass would be like. Not what you would change about the liturgy (which has a structure given by the Church), but your inner attitude: how would you enter the church? what would you offer at the offertory? how would you listen to the readings? how would you receive Communion? how would you leave? What would you carry with you?

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

D. Liturgical Treasure Hunt

This week, go to your parish church (or any Catholic church) outside of Mass and look for these items. As you find each one, write what it is and what it signifies:

  1. The tabernacle: ________________________________________________
  2. The sanctuary lamp: ________________________________________________
  3. The holy water font: ________________________________________________
  4. The ambo (lectern): ________________________________________________
  5. The baptismal font: ________________________________________________
  6. The Paschal candle (if present): ________________________________________________

Which of these did you already know about? Which was new to you?

 

 

4. Memory Verse

“Where two or three are gathered in my name, there am I in the midst of them.”

— Matthew 18:20

Going deeper: Also read Revelation 5:11-14, which describes the heavenly liturgy. What images stand out to you? Write one verse or phrase that struck you:

 

 

5. Prayer for This Week

Lord Jesus, eternal High Priest,
you who celebrated the first Mass at the Last Supper
and sealed it with your blood on the cross,
open my eyes to see what really happens in every liturgy.
Let me go to Mass not out of habit, but out of love.
Make me not a spectator, but a living member of your Body.
When I sing the Holy, Holy, Holy, make me truly join
the song of angels and saints in heaven.
And when I leave the church,
let me carry your presence to every person I meet this week.
Amen.

Preparation practice: This Sunday, before you leave for Mass, sit down for 2 minutes. Read the Gospel reading for that day (find it online or in a missal). Ask the Holy Spirit to help you hear what he wants to say to you. After Mass, write one thing that stayed with you:

 

 

 

6. Commitment for the Week

Choose at least one concrete commitment to live out this week. Check the one you choose:

I will arrive at Mass 5 minutes early this Sunday. No rushing. I'll sit in silence and prepare my heart before it begins.

I will do the “Liturgical Treasure Hunt” (Activity D) — visit a church and find all six items, then research anything I didn't understand.

I will pay attention to my posture at Mass this Sunday: when do I stand, sit, kneel? Why? Look up what each posture means and write it here.

I will not receive Communion on autopilot this Sunday. Before receiving, I will say silently: “Lord, I am not worthy that you should enter under my roof, but only say the word and my soul shall be healed.”

I will invite someone to come to Mass with me this Sunday — a friend, a family member who hasn't been in a while, or someone who has never experienced a Catholic liturgy.

My personal commitment (write it in your own words if you want something different):

 

 

At the end of the week, review: Did I keep my commitment? What changed in me by doing it? If I didn't keep it, what got in the way?

 

 

Print Workbook
